text: I want to do with you what spring does to cherry trees.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair, Poem 14

text: I love you as certain dark things are to be loved, in secret, between the shadow and the soul.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: One Hundred Love Sonnets, Sonnet XVII

text: I love you without knowing how, or when, or from where.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: One Hundred Love Sonnets, Sonnet XVII

text: I do not love you as if you were salt-rose, or topaz, or the arrow of carnations the fire shoots off.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: One Hundred Love Sonnets, Sonnet XVII

text: Tonight I can write the saddest lines.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair, Poem 20

text: Love is so short, forgetting is so long.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair, Poem 20

text: And it was at that age... Poetry arrived in search of me.
author: Pablo Neruda

text: You do not have to be good. You do not have to walk on your knees for a hundred miles through the desert repenting.
author: Mary Oliver

text: You only have to let the soft animal of your body love what it loves.
author: Mary Oliver

text: Tell me, what is it you plan to do with your one wild and precious life?
author: Mary Oliver

text: Instructions for living a life: Pay attention. Be astonished. Tell about it.
author: Mary Oliver

text: Someone I loved once gave me a box full of darkness. It took me years to understand that this, too, was a gift.
author: Mary Oliver

text: When it's over, I want to say: all my life I was a bride married to amazement.
author: Mary Oliver
source: New and Selected Poems, 'When Death Comes'

text: Attention is the beginning of devotion.
author: Mary Oliver

text: I don't want to end up simply having visited this world.
author: Mary Oliver
source: New and Selected Poems, 'When Death Comes'

text: Be patient toward all that is unsolved in your heart and try to love the questions themselves.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Letters to a Young Poet, Letter Four

text: Live the questions now. Perhaps you will then gradually, without noticing it, live along some distant day into the answer.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Letters to a Young Poet, Letter Four

text: We need, in love, to practice only this: letting each other go.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Beauty is nothing but the beginning of terror, which we are still just able to endure.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke

text: Perhaps all the dragons in our lives are princesses who are only waiting to see us act, just once, with beauty and courage.
author: Rainer Maria Rilke
source: Letters to a Young Poet, Letter Eight

text: i carry your heart with me (i carry it in my heart)
author: E. E. Cummings
source: 95 Poems, 'i carry your heart with me'

text: i like my body when it is with your body. It is so quite new a thing.
author: E. E. Cummings
source: Tulips and Chimneys, '[i like my body when it is with your]'

text: I am large, I contain multitudes.
author: Walt Whitman

text: I celebrate myself, and sing myself, and what I assume you shall assume.
author: Walt Whitman

text: Tread softly because you tread on my dreams.
author: W. B. Yeats
source: The Wind Among the Reeds, 'Aedh Wishes for the Cloths of Heaven'

text: I have spread my dreams under your feet.
author: W. B. Yeats
source: The Wind Among the Reeds, 'Aedh Wishes for the Cloths of Heaven'

text: Caring for myself is not self-indulgence, it is self-preservation, and that is an act of political warfare.
author: Audre Lorde

text: When I dare to be powerful, to use my strength in the service of my vision, then it becomes less and less important whether I am afraid.
author: Audre Lorde

text: Your silence will not protect you.
author: Audre Lorde
source: Sister Outsider, 'The Transformation of Silence into Language and Action'

text: The erotic is a measure between the beginnings of our sense of self and the chaos of our strongest feelings.
author: Audre Lorde

text: I am, I am, I am.
author: Sylvia Plath

text: Out of the ash I rise with my red hair, and I eat men like air.
author: Sylvia Plath

text: Someone will remember us, I say, even in another time.
author: Sappho

text: Green, how I want you green. Green wind. Green branches.
author: Federico García Lorca

text: Where the mind is without fear and the head is held high.
author: Rabindranath Tagore

text: Let me not pray to be sheltered from dangers, but to be fearless in facing them.
author: Rabindranath Tagore

text: Nothing can ever happen twice. In consequence, the sorry fact is that we arrive here improvised and leave without the chance to practice.
author: Wisława Szymborska
source: Calling Out to Yeti, 'Nothing Twice', trans. Stanisław Barańczak & Clare Cavanagh

text: We shall not cease from exploration, and the end of all our exploring will be to arrive where we started and know the place for the first time.
author: T. S. Eliot

text: April is the cruelest month, breeding lilacs out of the dead land.
author: T. S. Eliot

text: What we call the beginning is often the end. And to make an end is to make a beginning. The end is where we start from.
author: T. S. Eliot

text: Out beyond ideas of wrongdoing and rightdoing, there is a field. I'll meet you there.
author: Rumi
source: The Essential Rumi, trans. Coleman Barks

text: What you seek is seeking you.
author: Rumi
source: The Essential Rumi, trans. Coleman Barks

text: Lovers don't finally meet somewhere. They're in each other all along.
author: Rumi
source: The Essential Rumi, trans. Coleman Barks

text: The minute I heard my first love story, I started looking for you, not knowing how blind that was.
author: Rumi
source: Open Secret, trans. John Moyne & Coleman Barks

text: I, with a deeper instinct, choose a man who compels my strength, who makes enormous demands on me, who does not doubt my courage or my toughness.
author: Anaïs Nin

text: Wait, for now. Distrust everything if you have to. But trust the hours.
author: Galway Kinnell

text: I have walked through many lives, some of them my own.
author: Stanley Kunitz

text: An honorable human relationship is a process, delicate, violent, often terrifying to both persons involved, of refining the truths they can tell each other.
author: Adrienne Rich
source: On Lies, Secrets, and Silence, 'Women and Honor'

text: Lying is done with words, and also with silence.
author: Adrienne Rich
source: On Lies, Secrets, and Silence, 'Women and Honor'

text: All the new thinking is about loss. In this it resembles all the old thinking.
author: Robert Hass

text: In a field I am the absence of field.
author: Mark Strand

text: We look at the world once, in childhood. The rest is memory.
author: Louise Glück

text: Two roads diverged in a wood, and I— I took the one less traveled by, and that has made all the difference.
author: Robert Frost

text: There is no greater agony than bearing an untold story inside you.
author: Maya Angelou
source: I Know Why the Caged Bird Sings

text: If you wanna fly, you got to give up the shit that weighs you down.
author: Toni Morrison

text: You have the right to work, but never to the fruit of work.
author: Krishna

text: On this path no effort is wasted, no gain is ever reversed; even a little of this practice will shelter you from great sorrow.
author: Krishna

text: When meditation is mastered, the mind is unwavering like the flame of a lamp in a windless place.
author: Krishna

text: From the unreal lead me to the real. From darkness lead me to light. From death lead me to immortality.
author: Brihadaranyaka Upanishad

text: won't you celebrate with me what i have shaped into a kind of life?
author: Lucille Clifton
source: The Book of Light, 'won't you celebrate with me'

text: Before you know what kindness really is you must lose things, feel the future dissolve in a moment like salt in a weakened broth.
author: Naomi Shihab Nye

text: We must risk delight. We can do without pleasure, but not delight. Not enjoyment.
author: Jack Gilbert
source: Refusing Heaven, 'A Brief for the Defense'

text: We must have the stubbornness to accept our gladness in the ruthless furnace of this world.
author: Jack Gilbert
source: Refusing Heaven, 'A Brief for the Defense'

text: It may be that when we no longer know what to do, we have come to our real work, and that when we no longer know which way to go, we have begun our real journey.
author: Wendell Berry

text: I come into the peace of wild things who do not tax their lives with forethought of grief.
author: Wendell Berry

text: Your pain is the breaking of the shell that encloses your understanding.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: Love one another, but make not a bond of love. Let it rather be a moving sea between the shores of your souls.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: Ever has it been that love knows not its own depth until the hour of separation.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: Work is love made visible.
author: Kahlil Gibran

text: Hope is the thing with feathers that perches in the soul.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: I dwell in Possibility — a fairer House than Prose.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: There's a certain Slant of light, winter afternoons, that oppresses, like the heft of cathedral tunes.
author: Emily Dickinson

text: The mass of men lead lives of quiet desperation.
author: Henry David Thoreau

text: Go confidently in the direction of your dreams. Live the life you have imagined.
author: Henry David Thoreau

text: What is poetry which does not save nations or people?
author: Czesław Miłosz

text: Forgiveness is the only reaction which does not merely re-act but acts anew and unexpectedly.
author: Hannah Arendt

text: You are the sky. Everything else — it's just the weather.
author: Pema Chödrön

text: It is what we have lost most beautifully that we hold most beautifully.
author: Anne Carson

text: What is description, after all, but encoded desire?
author: Mark Doty

text: I lay on him as if my body were the map of him.
author: Sharon Olds

text: What you have heard is true. I was in his house that night.
author: Carolyn Forché

text: Your absence has gone through me like thread through a needle. Everything I do is stitched with its color.
author: W. S. Merwin

text: I am not one of those who left the land to the mercy of its enemies.
author: Anna Akhmatova

text: My poems, like precious wines, will have their turn.
author: Marina Tsvetaeva

text: Keep Ithaka always in your mind. Arriving there is what you are destined for. But do not hurry the journey at all.
author: C. P. Cavafy

text: So little I said. Short days. Short days. Short nights. Short years.
author: Czesław Miłosz

text: To see a World in a Grain of Sand and a Heaven in a Wild Flower, hold Infinity in the palm of your hand and Eternity in an hour.
author: William Blake

text: The road of excess leads to the palace of wisdom.
author: William Blake
source: The Marriage of Heaven and Hell

text: Glory be to God for dappled things — for skies of couple-colour as a brinded cow.
author: Gerard Manley Hopkins

text: When you eventually see through the veils to how things really are, you will keep saying again and again, this is certainly not like we thought it was.
author: Rumi
source: The Essential Rumi, trans. Coleman Barks

text: This being human is a guest house. Every morning a new arrival.
author: Rumi
source: The Essential Rumi, 'The Guest House', trans. Coleman Barks

text: No man is an island, entire of itself; every man is a piece of the continent, a part of the main.
author: John Donne
source: Devotions Upon Emergent Occasions, Meditation XVII

text: Shall I compare thee to a summer's day? Thou art more lovely and more temperate.
author: William Shakespeare

text: Love is not love which alters when it alteration finds, or bends with the remover to remove.
author: William Shakespeare

text: A thing of beauty is a joy forever; its loveliness increases.
author: John Keats

text: I mean Negative Capability, that is, when a man is capable of being in uncertainties, mysteries, doubts.
author: John Keats
source: Letter to George and Tom Keats

text: We are all in the gutter, but some of us are looking at the stars.
author: Oscar Wilde

text: The present moment is filled with joy and happiness. If you are attentive, you will see it.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: If you are a poet, you will see clearly that there is a cloud floating in this sheet of paper.
author: Thich Nhat Hanh

text: A journey of a thousand miles begins with a single step.
author: Lao Tzu
source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 64, trans. Stephen Mitchell

text: Nothing in the world is as soft and yielding as water. Yet for dissolving the hard and inflexible, nothing can surpass it.
author: Lao Tzu
source: Tao Te Ching, Chapter 78, trans. Stephen Mitchell

text: Are you looking for me? I am in the next seat.
author: Kabir
source: The Kabir Book, trans. Robert Bly

text: The butterfly counts not months but moments, and has time enough.
author: Rabindranath Tagore

text: Silence is the language of God; all else is poor translation.
author: Rumi
source: The Essential Rumi, trans. Coleman Barks

text: O my friends, what can you tell me of love, whose pathways are filled with strangeness?
author: Mirabai
source: Mirabai: Ecstatic Poems, trans. Jane Hirshfield

text: We don't have to live great lives, we just have to understand and survive the ones we've got.
author: Andre Dubus III

text: The first hour was an hour of mercy.
author: Sharon Olds

text: Body of a woman, white hills, white thighs, you look like a world, lying in surrender.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair, Poem 1

text: Drunk with pines and long kisses, like summer I steer the fast sailboat of the roses, bent towards the death of the thin day.
author: Pablo Neruda
source: Twenty Love Poems and a Song of Despair, Poem 12

text: Listen, are you breathing just a little, and calling it a life?
author: Mary Oliver
source: West Wind, 'Have You Ever Tried to Enter the Long Black Branches?'

text: Between the conscious and the unconscious, the mind has put up a swing.
author: Kabir
source: The Kabir Book, trans. Robert Bly

text: He was my North, my South, my East and West, my working week and my Sunday rest.
author: W. H. Auden

text: I do not know which to prefer, the beauty of inflections or the beauty of innuendoes, the blackbird whistling or just after.
author: Wallace Stevens
source: Harmonium, 'Thirteen Ways of Looking at a Blackbird'

text: Let be be finale of seem.
author: Wallace Stevens

text: The thief left it behind: the moon at my window.
author: Ryōkan
source: Dewdrops on a Lotus Leaf, trans. John Stevens

text: An old silent pond. A frog jumps into the pond— Splash! Silence again.
author: Matsuo Bashō

text: Lay down these words before your mind like rocks. placed solid, by hands in choice of place.
author: Gary Snyder

text: How marvelous and sacred a wet leaf is, freshly grown!
author: Robert Bly
